posts.author: Michał Rukszan
posts.title: My Brother's American Adventure (and How I Survived Without Him)
posts.excerpt: While my brother was busy living the American dream—from soccer in Arkansas to Spring Break in Mexico—I was back home in Poland navigating the bittersweet reality of 8th-grade stress, tricky time zones for gaming, and the glorious (yet lonely) luxury of having two bedrooms all to myself.
posts.body: My older brother went on a high school exchange to the USA when he was 17. He lived in Arkansas, attended an American high school, and even played for the school soccer team. During his exchange, he traveled a lot – he visited Los Angeles, Florida, Las Vegas, and went on a cruise to Mexico for Spring Break! To be honest, when he left, I didn’t care that much. I didn’t even say a proper goodbye. Just as I expected, the time flew by really fast, but there were moments when it slowed down and I really missed him. I am used to being alone, but this time I felt truly lonely. Knowing he was just a phone call away wasn't the same. I missed his support the most when I was in the 8th grade. I was extremely stressed about my final exams. My parents were there for me, but my brother had already passed these exams and could have given me the best advice. Somehow, I managed to do it all without him and I survived! We sometimes called each other to play video games together. However, it wasn't easy because of the 7-hour time difference. When it was 7:00 PM here in Poland, it was only noon in Arkansas. Since he had his own school schedule, we could only play on weekends or holidays. But there was one huge advantage to his absence! I had both of our rooms entirely to myself. I used one room as my gaming and studying area, and the other one just for sleeping and keeping my things. It honestly made my life so much easier during that time! Here is a picture of my brother with his friends from his favorite AP History class. You can see his school building in the background. This is the amazing view from his Spring Break cruise. Just look at this beautiful beach in Mexico! And this is him in Las Vegas, standing in front of the famous Sphere with his host Mom. The best part is that she is coming to visit us in Warsaw this summer!

posts.author: Michał Rukszan
posts.title: A Bittersweet Birthday at the Championships
posts.excerpt: On June 28th, which happened to be my birthday, I took part in one of the most important competitions of my life. It was the Polish Championships in climbing, held in Bytom.
posts.body: On June 28th, which happened to be my birthday, I took part in one of the most important competitions of my life. It was the Polish Championships in climbing, held in Bytom. I traveled there the day before with my dad, my brother, and Ziemek, another climber competing in my junior category. Ziemek and I were lucky to get free accommodation in a hotel thanks to funding for athletes. On Friday evening, my brother, Ziemek, and I went for a walk to meet other competitors and my coach, who were staying in a different hotel. The path led through a forest, and the late June weather was perfect for an evening walk. A beautiful sunset view during our walk to see the coach. When we met the coach, we talked about the competition. He advised me not to worry too much and not to stress because stress would only make things worse. Despite his advice, I was very stressed the next day. I tried my best during the bouldering competition, but unfortunately, it seemed I wasn't prepared enough yet. Out of 5 boulders, I managed to complete only 3 and reach one "zone". I ended up in 18th place. To get to the finals, you need to be in the top 8, so I didn't qualify this time. This is me during the competition, trying my hardest on the wall. The next day, I had another competition, this time in lead climbing. Things didn't go according to plan either. Halfway up the first route, my foot slipped, even though I felt I could have climbed it to the very top. On the second route, I couldn't focus properly because I was still thinking about my mistake on the first one. It ruined my chances of getting into the finals again. Sad after the start. I knew I could have done much better. It was a tough birthday weekend, but it was also a valuable experience for future competitions.

posts.author: Michał Rukszan
posts.title: Epic Summer Road Trip: Rocks, Glaciers, and a Close Call!
posts.excerpt: Early in July 2025, my dad, my brother, and I went on an amazing summer road trip. Our first stop was Zillertal in Austria. We camped there for two days because it was very close to the climbing area.
posts.body:
![](/images/termy.jpg)

![](/images/wloskauliczka.jpg)

Early in July 2025, my dad, my brother, and I went on an amazing summer road trip. Our first stop was Zillertal in Austria. We camped there for two days because it was very close to the climbing area.



I wanted to try a boulder called "King of Queens" (grade 7b+). The rocks were right next to a river, and getting to them was a real challenge! The river was high and freezing cold. We had to figure out how to carry our backpacks and crash pads across the water without getting them wet. My dad and brother didn't feel like climbing that day, so I was trying the boulder alone. After about ten attempts, I finally did it! It is still my best outdoor bouldering send ever, and I am really proud of it.



\[Insert Photo 1 here]

Me taking a well-deserved rest on the grass in Zillertal after sending the boulder.



Next, we drove to Switzerland to a famous climbing area called Magic Wood. I spent two days there trying a boulder called "Intermezzo". On the first day, I felt great. The conditions were good, and I figured out all the moves, but I just couldn't link them together to finish the whole route. I came back the next day full of hope, but the conditions were terrible. Because it's such a popular place, the holds were super slippery. I kept falling off, and sadly, I had to give up because we had to travel to our next destination.



\[Insert Photo 2 here]

A cool hotel building we saw while sightseeing in St. Moritz.



After a relaxing day in St. Moritz, we went for a huge hike up the Diavolezza mountain (2978 meters above sea level). The hike was long and tiring, but the views were totally worth it.



\[Insert Photo 5 here]

Here is a picture from our hike up the rocky ridge of Diavolezza.



When we were close to the top, something really scary happened. A thunderstorm was approaching. My hair under my helmet started to stand up from static electricity, I suddenly got a headache, and the rocks around us began making a weird buzzing sound. At first, I didn't know what it meant, but my dad realized we were in huge danger. We quickly rushed to the summit and then started running down. We descended safely by walking across a glacier back to the trail.



\[Insert Photo 6 here]

Descending the mountain via the glacier. It was an intense experience!



The next day, we crossed the border into Italy to see the beautiful Lake Como.



\[Insert Photo 4 here]

A beautiful view of Lake Como with a classic Italian boat.



\[Insert Photo 7 here]

We also walked through these charming, empty Italian streets at night.



In the evening, we went to Italian thermal baths. After all that bouldering and running away from a storm, relaxing in the hot water was exactly what we needed. The day after that, we started our journey back home to Poland.



\[Insert Photo 3 here]

My brother and I chilling in the glowing blue water of the thermal baths.

![](/images/zillertal.jpg)

![](/images/stmoritz.jpg)

![](/images/diavolezza.jpg)

![](/images/lodowiec.jpg)

![](/images/como.jpg)
